S1: Nordisk Välfärdsforskning Nordic Welfare Research article Research publication Work-Aged Family Caregivers’ Experiences in Severe Neurological Diseases and Sustainability in Future Local Healthcare Workforce: A Qualitative Metasynthesis Randi Martinsen Associate professor, Department of Health and Nursing Sciences, University of Inland Norway, Elverum, Norway randi.martinsen@inn.no https://orcid.org/0000-0002-4760-2055 Ellen J. Svendsboe Associate professor, Department of Health- and Care Sciences, Western Norway University of Applied Sciences, Stord, Norway ellen.svendsbo@hvl.no https://orcid.org/0000-0002-2864-4057 Merete Røthing Associate professor, Department of Health and Care Sciences, Western Norway University of Applied Sciences, Haugesund, Norway merete-r@haugnett.no https://orcid.org/0000-0003-0443-0967 Kristin Ådnøy Eriksen Associate professor, Department of Health and Care Sciences, Western Norway University of Applied Sciences, Haugesund, Norway kristin.eriksen@hvl.no https://orcid.org/0000-0003-4142-1074 Submitted: 1 August 2023 Accepted: 9 October 2024 Published: 29 November 2024 Abstract Family caregivers play an important role as an unpaid part in the health care workforce in the communities.
S2: With- out family caregivers’ contribution, sustainability in the health care workforce may be severely challenged.
S3: This metasynthesis identifies the experiences of work-aged family caregivers, related to care for persons living with Amy- otrophic Lateral Sclerosis, Multiple Sclerosis, Huntington’s Disease and Parkinson’s Disease to uphold a sustainable health care workforce.
S4: Eleven original qualitative studies concerning family caregiving in severe, degenerative neuro- logical diseases were included and analysed based on an elaborated, four steps strategy.
S5: The results revealed the three following themes: The practical and emotional impact on family caregiver, Balancing own needs and family obligations and A price to pay for family caregivers providing long-term care.
S6: These findings implicate that family caregivers’con- tribution depends on tailored information from the health care regarding the disease and the development of the dis- ease.
S7: Work-aged family caregivers’needs should be clarified and valued, regarding their own personal time, economic situation, social activity, work, and family situation.
S8: The health authorities should discuss the financial framework for family caregivers’contribution and role.
S9: It is further necessary that the health care workforce invites to dialogue about Copyright © 2024 Author(s).
S10: This is an open access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons CC-BY 4.0 License (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/).
S12: 4-2024, p. 300–313 ISSN online: 2464-4161 DOI: https://doi.org/10.18261/nwr.9.4.3 RESEARCH PUBLICATION prerequisites for health care and how health care tasks could be distributed to uphold a sustainable health care both for the work force and the work-aged family caregivers in the future.
S13: Keywords family caregiver role, health care workforce, metasynthesis, severe degenerative neurological diseases, sustainability Background The healthcare work systems will depend on collaboration between family caregivers and health professionals in the future (Verbakel, 2018).
S14: Most family caregivers would like to sup- port and provide the best care for their loved ones.
S15: However, many experience it difficult to take care of themselves when family members need long-term care (Schulz & Sherwood, 2008).
S16: Although they include a small part of the healthcare system, the family caregivers are having a major role in home long-term care.
S17: In Norway, family members already provide for 50% of caregiving in the communities (Hjemås et al., 2019), which represent almost the same amount of work years as health professionals in primary healthcare (The Norwegian Government, 2020).
S18: This makes healthcare services complex, challenging the sustainability of the healthcare workforce (Hjemås et al., 2019).
S19: The World Health Organization (WHO) has agreed on a programme for sustainable development within health and well-being.
S20: The programme has practical and financial con- sequences for healthcare systems, including adapting services to individual needs, access to resources for all, and the distribution of responsibilities among health professionals, fam- ily caregivers and patients (World Health Organization, 2016a, 2016b).
S21: Due to better treat- ment methods and technology, it is possible for most patients to stay at home despite severe and chronic illness conditions.
S22: Individuals living with the largest group of severe, degener- ative, neurological diseases such as Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS), Multiple Sclerosis (MS), Huntington’s Disease (HD), or Parkinson’s Disease (PD) are living with a demanding and long-lasting care trajectory without the possibility of a medical cure.
S23: With an increas- ing need for informal care in private homes, and the demand that healthcare systems should adapt services to individual needs, it is important to understand the experiences of work- aged family caregivers due to their major role in long-term care combined with working out- side home.
S24: On this basis, this study explores the preconditions for sustainable and health- promoting collaborations between family caregivers and healthcare professionals.
S25: It can be useful to understand this collaboration considering how the systems interconnect, ranging from the immediate surrounding to the broad societal structures (i.e.
S26: micro-, meso- and macro-level) (Van Wijk et al., 2019).
S27: This study refers to persons with degenerative neuro- logic diseases and family caregivers defined as the micro-level, health professionals as the meso-level, and health authorities and politicians as the macro-level in the healthcare work- force system.
S28: Family caregivers risk developing health problems of their own due to being overloaded with care burdens; reported symptoms are insomnia, increased use of medication, social isolation, depressive symptoms and, for some, sick leave from work (Aamodt et al., 2023; Adelman et al., 2014; Yang et al., 2024).
S29: Caregiving tasks represent a barrier to fulfilling their own work obligations.
S30: Work outside the home and social activities with family and friends are important for individual well-being (Adelman et al., 2014; Moudatsou et al., 2021; Svendsboe et al., 2018; Tzounis et al., 2017).
S31: Collaborating with the healthcare services is complex due to family caregivers’different expectations, wishes, values and opportunities for involvement.
S32: It is challenging to make 301 NORDISK VÄLFÄRDSFORSKNING | NORDIC WELFARE RESEARCH | VOLUME 9 | NO.
S33: 4-2024 good plans for the care, as possibilities for treatment, progression and severity of the illness will vary across diagnoses (Bernell & Howard, 2016).
S34: An increasing elderly population in need of care may also challenge the possibility of family caregivers to combine their care- giving role with work outside the home (Gautun & Hernes, 2006).
S35: Research has suggested that family caregivers’ status should be assessed continuously and recommend that family caregivers should be directed to appropriate resources for support (Collins & Swartz, 2011; Yang et al., 2024).
S36: In this study, the term family caregiver refers to informal caregivers/carers, meaning a fam- ily member – i.e., partner, husband, wife, or adult children – involved in unpaid caregiving tasks and in addition affected in some way by the individuals’ need for long-term home care caused by severe, progressive, degenerative neurological diseases.
S37: Aim The aim was to identify the experiences of the work-aged family caregivers related to care for persons living with severe, degenerative, neurological diseases.
S38: Research Questions What are the needs identified by work-aged family caregivers to be able to care for persons living with neurological diseases in their homes?
S39: What are the preconditions for a sustainable cooperation between the work-aged family caregivers and the healthcare professionals?
S40: Method Metasynthesis is described as a method for development of knowledge from already existing original research (Atkins et al., 2008; Barnett-Page & Thomas, 2009; France et al., 2014; Kinn et al., 2013).
S41: Utilizing and upcycling knowledge (i.e., to reuse results from original studies) from existing research provides an opportunity to elaborate on previous knowledge to a higher value than the results in the primary studies (Malterud, 2019; Noblit & Hare, 1988).
S42: The Searching and Analysis Process A research librarian supported the searching process.
S43: The initial database search in Embase, PsycInfo and Cinahl resulted in 507 articles.
S44: The supplementing search in Scopus and Med- line resulted in 97 articles (see Figure 1).
S45: This search resulted in atotal of 604 articles relevant for further screening by title and abstract, which were grouped in four subsets of 151 arti- cles by the librarian.
S46: The subsets were distributed to the four researchers (RM, EJS, MR and KÅE) withthe purpose of individually identifying whether titles and abstracts met the inclu- sion criteria as described under.
S47: A total of 59 articles were read in full and assessed for eligi- bility.
S48: Out of this, 48 articles were excluded.
S49: The eleven articles remaining were included in the metasynthesis (see Table 1).
S50: Mesh terms were family caregiver, informal caregiver, informal care, informal carer, chronic disease, home care, needs, support, well-being, collaboration, qualitative design.
S51: Inclusion criteria: • Diseases: ALS, MS, HD and PD • Adult family caregiver (18–67 years) • Caregiving in private home 302 RANDI MARTINSEN, ELLEN J. SVENDSBOE, MERETE RØTHING AND KRISTIN ÅDNØY ERIKSEN • Original studies with a qualitative design • Results related to caregivers’ needs, wishes, reflections or experiences on having a life of their own Exclusion criteria: • Children aged under eighteen in caregiver role • Other diagnosis than the four selected • Studies with mixed informants as health professionals and family caregivers • Studies with mixed research methods • Quantitative studies Studies screened by reading the abstracts (n=604) Articles identified through initial database search Embase (n=264) PsycINFO (n=83) Cinahl (n=238) Total (n=507) Removed duplicates (n=78) Supplementing search Scopus and Medline (n=102) Total (n=97) Removed duplicates (n=5) Studies included in the metasynthesis (n=11) Screening Included Studies excluded (n=48) according to the exclusion criteria x Children aged under 18 x Other diagnoses than ALS, MS, HD, PD x Studies with mixed informants (health personnel and family caregivers) x Studies with mixed methods x Quantitative studies Idenfication Full-text studies assessed for eligibility (n=59) Studies excluded (n=545) Figure 1.

S74: The analysis followed the following four steps inspired by Malterud (2019): 1: To give an overview of included articles: author(s), title, country, aim, design, main results.
S75: The articles were sorted into categories: a) included, b) excluded, and c) “not certain» (which included articles that had to be further reviewed).
S76: All articles were read, reviewed, and checked for quality and eligibility by two separate researchers (RM and KÅE/MR and EJS).
S77: All the included articles were assessed by means of a checklist, and were found to be valid, with a clear purpose, suitable method, described selection strategy, account of background factors, ethical assessments, clear results and usefulness for practice (The Norwegian Elec- tronic Health Library, 2023; Critical Appraisal Skills Programme, 2018).
S78: 2: Meaning units and metaphors elucidating our purpose for the metasynthesis were iden- tified.
S79: Relevant themes and metaphors from each study were listed and structured.
S80: All four researchers started this part of work individually for a later common discussion of how we interpreted and understood the meaning units.
S81: 3: Metaphors and meaning units from the results across the primary studies were system- atically organized.
S82: All four researchers contributed to discussions of understanding similar- ities or differences in use of terms.
S83: In step 4, we identified themes from the analysis process and a draft was prepared by one of the authors (MR).
S84: We then created «headlines» representable for our understanding of the findings.
S85: The synthesising of the articles, and sections giving meaning ended up in three themes as revealed in the results section.
S86: Authors Title Country Diagnosis N Results 305 NORDISK VÄLFÄRDSFORSKNING | NORDIC WELFARE RESEARCH | VOLUME 9 | NO.
S87: 4-2024 Results Family caregivers described a complex caregiving situation characterised by lack of informa- tion, unpredictability of care responsibilities and changes in relationships.
S88: The complexity emphasised feelings of helplessness reinforced by broken agreements with healthprofession- als.
S89: These described results are summarised in the three following themes: «The practical and emotional impact on family caregiver»; «Balancing own needs and family obligations»; and «A price to pay for family caregivers providing long-term care».
S90: The Practical and Emotional Impact on Family Caregiver A lack of knowledge and information enforced feelings of helplessness.
S91: Family caregivers lacked information about the severity of the disease and illness trajectory (Aubeeluck et al., 2012) and the family caregivers sometimes felt that they were left with total uncertainty.
S92: Family caregivers found patients’cognitive problems and changes in behaviour frustrating without the knowledge to understand it.
S93: Family caregivers also struggled to find informa- tion about symptoms, duration, and answers to understand changes in behaviour in the person living with the neurological disease.
S94: This impacted family life and relationships.
S95: For some, the conflict regarding changes in the day-to-day living resulted in fragmented families (Cipolletta & Amicucci, 2015; Rothing et al., 2015).
S96: Family caregivers found cognitive prob- lems and changes in behaviour frustrating, without the knowledge to understand it.
S97: This not knowing adds complications and challenges to daily life (Moberg et al., 2017; Rollero, 2016; Rothing et al., 2015).
S98: The search for information was experienced as an unnecessary, time-consuming responsibility added to the burden of their daily care tasks (Aubeeluck et al., 2012).
S99: Family caregivers were not prepared for changes in the person living with neurological disease’s needs nor the duration of demanding care tasks (Cross et al., 2019).
S100: They found it demanding to be thrown into a situation involving severe illness and regular care needs.
S101: They never knew in advance what would come next.
S102: It was described as facing an unknown enormity of care burden.
S103: Home care visits from the healthcare services did not seem to reduce the burden of being the overall responsible person.
S104: They had to deal with trouble- some and unavoidable situations (Weisser et al., 2015).
S105: One family caregiver for a patient inflicted with MS expressed the practical and emotional impact on a family caregiver as fol- lows: “… [it] feels like there is always something to do, phone calls to make, appointments to drive to…!
S106: Is it always going to be like this?» (Cross et al., 2019).
S107: Balancing Own Needs and Family Obligations Family caregivers described that, over time, caregiving tasks turned their life upside down.
S108: They described a constant situation of balancing the caregiver role with other daily fam- ily obligations.
S109: This had consequences for the quality of the relationships between family members.
S110: Family caregivers felt caregiving as a duty and had no real choice or alternative to balance duty and autonomy.
S111: Despite complexity and uncertainty, the family caregivers were motivated to provide care as expressed in the following quote: «It was not a real choice whether to help.
S112: I felt it like an inner drive to help» (Moberg et al., 2017).
S113: Some caregivers blamed themselves for not always doing their best (Leiknes & Høye, 2012).
S114: They felt guilty about talking about their own worries, as this implied not being loyal to their loved one.
S115: In families with HD, family caregivers had feelings of wanting to run away from it all (Aubeeluck et al., 2012).
S116: They tried to maintain the ability to keep up with their own work.
S117: Some experienced that as a demanding exercise.
S118: Across the original studies, the family caregivers described how the balance between their own needs and the patients’ need 306 RANDI MARTINSEN, ELLEN J. SVENDSBOE, MERETE RØTHING AND KRISTIN ÅDNØY ERIKSEN for care was skewed and challenged the daily living (Aubeeluck et al., 2012; Rothing et al., 2015; Weisser et al., 2015) revealed in this representative quote: “It is our faith to do our best – with increasing losses» (Anderson et al., 2019).
S119: The family caregivers also described ambivalence between seeking help and being invaded by health professionals entering their home (Weisser et al., 2015).
S120: They stated that health professionals sometimes arrived too late due to a tight schedule, making it difficult to trust that appointments would be kept (Anderson et al., 2019).
S121: Another dilemma was about being an informal caregiver who felt expected to act as a professional.
S122: They experienced that no one cared for them and felt that the healthcare services expected them to perform medical procedures, although they were not comfortable having that responsibility.
S123: The unclarified responsibilities in their role and the responsibilities were overwhelming in many ways.
S124: An illustrative quote is: “Iam useless with equipment, absolutely useless with mechanical things, so lifts and those kinds of devices intimidate me» (Anderson et al., 2019).
S125: A Price to Pay for Family Caregivers Providing Long-Term Care The new life situation being a carer to a person living with neurologic disease by severe long-lasting illness, resulted in life changes for family caregivers.
S126: Even though some fam- ily caregivers described new dimensions of their relationship as positive, it was the experi- ence of losses that dominated (Aubeeluck et al., 2012).
S127: A loss of social contact and support resulted in isolation for both the person living with neurological disease and the family caregiver.
S128: Some experienced that in the beginning, friends provided support, but as time went by, the support diminished.
S129: Organising transport or other specific care tasks related to care needs was sometimes exhausting (Rollero, 2016).
S130: The quality of relationships among family members became poorer and young family caregivers needed extended support and understanding of their complex caring situation (Jonzon & Goodwin, 2012).
S131: The possibility for breaks, relief from caregiving and a sense of freedom with individual needs gradually decreased for the caregivers (Anderson et al., 2019; Aubeeluck et al., 2012; Rollero, 2016; Rothing et al., 2015).
S132: They felt that their life was put on hold and emotional difficulties like anxiety became problematic (Rothing et al., 2015).
S133: They also mentioned that the relation- ship between them as a couple gradually changed.
S134: The love turned into a more care-based relationship (Jonzon & Goodwin, 2012; Rollero, 2016).
S135: The family caregivers also described feelings of being inadequate for the caregiving responsibilities required for patients, result- ing in anxiety and negligence of their own health (Aubeeluck et al., 2012; Dekawaty et al., 2019; Rollero, 2016; Rothing et al., 2015) as illustrated in the following quote: “I try to stay healthy.
S136: Sleep as much as Ican and go for walks alone.
S138: Sometimes, I find time for a little trip, alone or with my children.
S140: I need something to look forward to and something nice to think of later.
S141: I have to, if not, I know I will be depressed» (Rothing et al., 2015).
S142: Discussion Findings across the original studies underscore the experiences of vulnerability and risk of health problems, which in turn threaten stability and continuity in family caregivers’contri- butions as an informal part of a healthcare workforce.
S143: In the first part of the discussion, the family caregivers’experiences of their role are highlighted.
S144: The second part of the discussion reveals the implication for practice, meaning significance for sustainability in the healthcare workforce.
S145: 307 NORDISK VÄLFÄRDSFORSKNING | NORDIC WELFARE RESEARCH | VOLUME 9 | NO.
S146: 4-2024 Living in an Ongoing Role Conflict Living with the dilemma between performing the role of a family caregiver at the same time as attempting to live an everyday life, is experienced as a personal and individual concern.
S147: However, being at risk of neglecting one’s own health and developing health problems such as isolation, depression, and sleeping problems is not only an individual problem on a micro- level, as the healthcare workforce depends on family caregiver efforts.
S148: Individuals who take part in social activities outside the home will score higher on measures of mental health and indicators of well-being (Gil-González et al., 2023).
S149: The findings in this study show that family caregivers experience living with an ongoing worrying and ruminating during their day-to-day living.
S150: This strain leads to a possible risk for developing health problems.
S151: Being at risk of developing own health problems is not surprising as recent research has shown that family caregivers experience burdens, also in other illness care courses than the four neuro- logical diseases focused on in this study (Adelman et al., 2014; Appleton et al., 2018; Chong et al., 2023; Svendsboe et al., 2018; Tzounis et al., 2017).
S152: The family caregivers report that they are motivated by their relationship and love for the affected family member, and they wish to support and care for their loved ones (Aubeeluck et al., 2012).
S153: Grief caused by loss and change seems manageable over time and is perceived as a part of life.
S154: An autonomous motivation for family caregivers is found to be an impor- tant determinant for well-being (Dombestein et al., 2020).
S155: Thus, it makes sense to support the family caregivers in collaboration with health professionals.
S156: Health professionals can be aware of and address the issues of role balancing and offer support in issues linked to the caregivers’ongoing worrying need to be part of the collaboration.
S157: The healthcare workforce will rely on family caregivers’ efforts to provide home care services 24/7.
S158: This indicates a collaboration about practical caring tasks, but also that professionals in the healthcare work- force need to pay attention to the total strain in the lives of the caregivers.
S159: Recent studies show that having the ability to manage difficult thoughts and being acknowledged by par- ticipating in groups with peers are also found to be positive (Hjelle et al., 2023; Olesen et al., 2023).
S160: Despite diversity in severity of the four neurological diseases focused on in this study, the family caregivers experience that they need tailored information and knowledge by skilled health professionals.
S161: This is important for the family caregivers because their own life might be affected by caregiving responsibilities in a caregiving course (Corry et al., 2015).
S162: Developing Sustainable Healthcare The findings in this metasynthesis point to a need for health care considering family caregiv- ers’ vulnerability to develop their own health problems.
S163: This part of the discussion reveals suggestions for developing sustainable healthcare on the meso- and macro-levels.
S164: The healthcare workforce, representing the meso-level, should offer preconditions for caregiv- ing responsibilities which motivate for continuity and partnership by involving the families’ competence as an informal part and active user of a healthcare workforce (Patient and User Rights Act, 1999, § 3-3; The Norwegian Government, 2020).
S165: There is a need for stable, ade- quate, and adapted information and support.
S166: Information and support should therefore be a part of the health promotion strategy to promote individual health and well-being (World Health Organization, 1995).
S167: To fill the gaps between the guidelines and practice for interac- tion with family caregivers that are found (The Norwegian Government, 2020), it is required to provide necessary and relevant information that in turn might ease the family caregivers’ burden and contribute to robust and long-lasting relationships as an important foundation for collaborations.
S168: Building trustful relationships between family caregivers and healthcare professionals over time means that all parties involved can fulfil their roles and expectations 308 RANDI MARTINSEN, ELLEN J. SVENDSBOE, MERETE RØTHING AND KRISTIN ÅDNØY ERIKSEN without compromising their own health.
S169: Coordinated care processes and updated commu- nication between those involved is therefore essential (Friedman & Tong, 2020), as informa- tion sharing may ensure the basis for shared decision-making (Elwyn et al., 2012; Friedman & Tong, 2020).
S170: Collaboration is a complex process that requires motivation from everyone involved, in addition to optimal organisational structures of healthcare service units (Braithwaite et al., 2015).
S171: As medical or technical care is experienced as falling outside the scope of the respon- sibilities of the family caregivers, there is a need for clarification of roles to ensure that caregiving over time is endured.
S172: The complexity of the work situation for healthcare profes- sionals is known (Holmen et al., 2020), meaning that the clarification of the roles between partners and a clarification of decision-making values are essential (Wittenberg et al., 2018).
S173: To acknowledge the caregivers as a resource in the partnership (The Norwegian Govern- ment, 2020), the family caregivers need to be supported by health professionals holding a specialist knowledge (Flemming et al., 2021) that manage to include the caregivers’ loss and grief (Leidl et al., 2023; Marterre et al., 2022) in a way that makes it possible for them to live their own lives in well-being and combine the family caregiver role with education and work outside the home.
S174: There are ongoing discussions in several countries about how to achieve a better balance between involved parties in different roles and establish sustainable healthcare services (Ver- bakel, 2018).
S175: These discussions must be continued (Jenhaug, 2018; World Health Organi- zation, 2016a).
S176: Educational support in care tasks (The Norwegian Government, 2020) must be included in the discussions.
S177: Moreover, on a macro-level, sustainability implies a discus- sion of financial priorities, the cost of sick leave for family caregivers, and welfare values in a society.
S178: Expecting a 24/7 caregiver role seems ambitious.
S179: Cost-effectiveness and consen- sus on how best to implement the most promising actions is suggested (Friedman & Tong, 2020).
S180: Providing for future professional healthcare is a concern related to human resources (Ricketts, 2011).
S181: A sustainable healthcare service system is no stronger than the weakest part in the level of micro-, meso- and macro-level, meaning that sustainability in the healthcare workforce depends on the efforts of all the involved parties (Van Wijk et al., 2019).
S182: Strength and Limitations The researchers are experienced from research and clinical practice of caregiving in neuro- logical diseases, dementia, and mental health, which can be considered a strength in inter- preting the analysis, discussions, and data synthesising.
S183: The researchers acknowledge that despite endeavours to remain critical and mindful, their motivation, sense of self and under- standing of the caregiver’s experiences may have shaped the findings.
S184: Further, it might be an advantage to have an interprofessional view of the results and discussion, giving a broader understanding of the findings in future synthesis.
S185: Regarding the findings, the themes are supplemented with quotes from the original stud- ies to underline the results.
S186: Two of the studies (Anderson et al., 2019; Leiknes & Høye, 2012) included spouses older than 67 years.
S187: However, both articles were discussed in the team and included in the metasynthesis because the findings were found to be relevant and important.
S188: The study is based on information from work-aged family caregivers in persons living with neurological diseases, and results are not directly transferable to caregivers of persons with other diagnoses.
S189: However, the long-term care trajectory, severity in change of roles in every- day life for family caregivers, and need for care at home, make it possible to some extent to transfer the findings to other comparable contexts.
S190: Including studies involving older people than 67 years of age could have given different findings.
S191: 309 NORDISK VÄLFÄRDSFORSKNING | NORDIC WELFARE RESEARCH | VOLUME 9 | NO.
S192: 4-2024 Conclusion Focusing on contextual factors using micro-, meso- and macro-levels clarifies how family caregivers are affected when living in a highly difficult and overburdened situation (Adel- man et al., 2014).
S193: At a micro-level, work-aged family caregivers’ roles need to be elabo- rated.
S194: Their contribution depends on tailored information from the healthcare workforce regarding the disease and the development of the disease.
S195: Further, the individual work-aged family caregivers’ needs should be clarified and valued, regarding their own personal time, economic situation, social activity, work, and family situation.
S196: At the meso-level, it is nec- essary that the healthcare workforce invites to a dialogue about prerequisites for healthcare and how healthcare tasks could be distributed.
S197: Health authorities, representing the macro- level, should discuss the financial framework for work-aged family caregivers’contribution and role, building their decision on scientific knowledge.
S198: The findings in this metasynthesis reveal the need for more research on how to maintain a sustainable healthcare workforce in the future.
S199: However, stronger cooperation between the micro-, meso- and macro-levels is crucial to develop a sustainable future for the families of persons living with long-lasting neurological diseases.
